ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes a practical combination—representing a document as the average of word embeddings while training with an explicit mask-out (dropout) corruption (eq.(1)) so that averaging is enforced during learning, and derives a data-dependent regularizer from a Taylor expansion (eq.(6)-(7)). These elements yield a useful, efficient variant distinct from Paragraph Vectors and from naive Word2Vec+AVG, and the authors empirically and analytically differentiate their approach (e.g., compare to Huang et al. 2012, Paragraph Vectors, and relate to dropout/denoising analyses). However, the core components (averaging embeddings, dropout/denoising, and related theoretical analyses) are largely existing techniques recombined rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, so the contribution is incremental rather than highly novel. | B(3): The paper combines known elements (averaging word embeddings, dropout/denoising corruption, negative sampling) into a new training setup that enforces averaged embeddings to serve as document vectors and analyzes the corruption as a data-dependent regularizer (Sec.3 and 3.1). The problem (document representation) is well-studied and the core idea is an incremental, pragmatic fusion rather than a radical new formulation; the authors do clearly distinguish from Paragraph Vectors, DEA and prior averaging work (Huang et al., 2012) and provide a non-trivial analytic justification, but the technique largely adapts existing methods rather than introducing wholly novel machinery.

These elements yield a useful, efficient variant distinct from Paragraph Vectors and from naive Word2Vec+AVG, and the authors empirically and analytically differentiate their approach (e.g., compare to Huang et al. 2012, Paragraph Vectors, and relate to dropout/denoising analyses). However, the core components (averaging embeddings, dropout/denoising, and related theoretical analyses) are largely existing techniques recombined rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, so the contribution is incremental rather than highly novel.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper combines known elements (averaging word embeddings, dropout/denoising corruption, negative sampling) into a new training setup that enforces averaged embeddings to serve as document vectors and analyzes the corruption as a data-dependent regularizer (Sec.3 and 3.1). The problem (document representation) is well-studied and the core idea is an incremental, pragmatic fusion rather than a radical new formulation; the authors do clearly distinguish from Paragraph Vectors, DEA and prior averaging work (Huang et al., 2012) and provide a non-trivial analytic justification, but the technique largely adapts existing methods rather than introducing wholly novel machinery.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow (Intro → Related Work/Notations → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (intro bullets listing Doc2VecC properties). Notation is largely defined in Section 2 and used consistently, and technical ideas (e.g. the corruption model and its regularization in Section 3.1) are explained clearly. Presentation suffers from formatting artifacts and duplicated/garbled text in the provided file (repeated sentences and LaTeX remnants near the end) and several figures/tables are only referenced (e.g. Figure 1, Tables 1–6) so their effectiveness cannot be judged here; these issues slightly reduce readability but do not obscure the paper’s core ideas. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized (Intro → Related Work/Notations → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early in the Introduction (the numbered list of desirable properties of Doc2VecC). Notation is introduced in Section 2 and used consistently through the method (equations (1)–(7)), and experimental descriptions refer to informative figures/tables (t-SNE visualizations, Tables 1/2/5) that support the claims. Presentation is marred only by some formatting/duplication and LaTeX artifacts in the provided text (repeated paragraphs and stray tokens at the end) and a few awkward long sentences, but these are minor and do not prevent understanding.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow (Intro → Related Work/Notations → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (intro bullets listing Doc2VecC properties). Notation is largely defined in Section 2 and used consistently, and technical ideas (e.g. the corruption model and its regularization in Section 3.1) are explained clearly. Presentation suffers from formatting artifacts and duplicated/garbled text in the provided file (repeated sentences and LaTeX remnants near the end) and several figures/tables are only referenced (e.g. Figure 1, Tables 1–6) so their effectiveness cannot be judged here; these issues slightly reduce readability but do not obscure the paper’s core ideas.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (Intro → Related Work/Notations → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early in the Introduction (the numbered list of desirable properties of Doc2VecC). Notation is introduced in Section 2 and used consistently through the method (equations (1)–(7)), and experimental descriptions refer to informative figures/tables (t-SNE visualizations, Tables 1/2/5) that support the claims. Presentation is marred only by some formatting/duplication and LaTeX artifacts in the provided text (repeated paragraphs and stray tokens at the end) and a few awkward long sentences, but these are minor and do not prevent understanding.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are supported by both a clear mathematical derivation (corruption as an unbiased mask, Taylor expansion of E[f(x̃)] yielding a trace(Σ Hessian) regularizer) and extensive empirical comparisons (IMDB, Wikipedia, SICK) against appropriate baselines. The derivations of the Hessian term and the variance (Σ diagonal = q/(1−q)·x_j^2) and the resulting data-dependent regularizer are algebraically consistent with the negative-sampling objective. Minor technical concerns remain: the Taylor (second-order) approximation is used without discussion of its accuracy for large corruption rates (they use q=0.9, yielding large variance), and experiments report no statistical significance tests (and one reported setting trains representations using the test set, though they also evaluate the proper train-only setting). These issues are relatively minor and do not overturn the main conclusions. | B(4): Overall the paper's claims are supported by clear derivations and a broad set of experiments against appropriate baselines (IMDB, Wikipedia, SICK, word-analogy). The mathematical derivation of the corruption-as-regularizer (second-order Taylor expansion about the corruption mean, diagonal variance q/(1−q) for binary BoW) and the Hessian-based regularizer form are standard and appear correct. Minor concerns: the Taylor approximation accuracy is not discussed (they use very large corruption q≈0.9, which can strain a second-order expansion), some implementation details (negative-sampling/unigram choice, exact training hyper‑parameters) and statistical significance of results are omitted. These are methodological shortcomings but do not overturn the main technical claims.

The derivations of the Hessian term and the variance (Σ diagonal = q/(1−q)·x_j^2) and the resulting data-dependent regularizer are algebraically consistent with the negative-sampling objective. These issues are relatively minor and do not overturn the main conclusions.
